Please describe your research career to date (1000 words)
My research career has involved developing skills in data science, health
services and qualitative research. To date, I have 12 academic publications, 12
conference abstracts, and have co-edited and published a book. The next stage
of my academic development is to train to become an expert in these
disciplines in my area of academic interest, invasive mechanical ventilation
(IMV) in critical care.
Research Interests
During my Academic Clinical Fellowship at Imperial College London, I developed
research interests and learned from academic supervisors and collaborators.
Background work has included a narrative review assessing ventilation practice
and decision-making (published in the British Journal of Anaesthesia); a national
survey assessing use of clinical decision support systems (CDSS) in intensive

secrc__001_p.text:
care; and preliminary work building a machine learning model using ventilation
data from a randomised controlled trial of over 200 patients with collaborators
at Aalborg University. I have developed skills in data science, completing a
Masters in Health Data Science at UCL while working full-time clinically, for
which I was awarded a Merit. I have learned to code in Python, SQL, Matlab,
Stata and R, and gained experience of statistics and machine learning. I am a
national coordinator of GEMINI, an international study evaluating ventilation
practice, through which I have experienced the Health Research Authority
approval process and collaborated with NHS Trusts to contribute to a database
of over 10,000 patients, which will act as a data repository for my PhD. The first
abstract from GEMINI will be presented at the American Thoracic Society
Conference in 2025.
I have developed collaborations with the Imperial-NIHR HealthTech Research
Centre, with support provided by methodologists, health economists and
implementation scientists; the Imperial Data Science Institute, to assist with
machine learning analysis and visualisation development; and Aalborg
University, drawing on their exper

secrc__001_q.text:
tise in CDSS and biomedical engineering. For
GEMINI, I have collaborated with the VentilaGroup in Spain. Additionally,
background work has involved collaboration with the Intensive Care Society and
the Paediatric Critical Care Society.
I have collaborated with patients through presentations workshops organised
by the Research Partner’s Group and the Patient Experience Research Centre at
Imperial. I have recruited a patient co-applicant and convened a Patient and
Family Advisory Group to advise on the project through the charities ICUSteps
and Voice. These experiences have refined the proposal, lay summary and
outcome measures.

secrc__001_r.text:
Education & Qualitative Research Experience
I have completed a Masters in Medical Education (UCL), achieving a Merit. Using
these skills, I developed my own teaching, winning prizes and being awarded
Fellowship of the Higher Education Academy. I developed the Health
Professions Education Masters course with Kings College London for healthcare
students in Somaliland, teaching virtually and in person; undertook a teaching
fellowship in Zambia, for which I was awarded several competitive travel
bursaries, delivering simulation and skills workshops, the impact of which have
been presented internationally; and co-written online modules for BMJ Learning
(“Challenges in Hospital Care: Sepsis – recognition and management in adults”)
and the Association of Anaesthetists (“Professional Practice in Resource Poor
Settings”). I co-edited and wrote several chapters for a textbook, “Surviving
Prescribing”, published by Cambridge University Press. I am on the committee
of two courses delivered through the Imperial School of Anaesthesia, the
Novice Course, and the Imperial Final Viva Course. Through the Masters, I
developed critical appraisal and qualitative analysis skills, and learned how to
crea

secrc__001_s.text:
te data collection tools, conduct interviews, and lead focus groups.
Leadership
I have undertaken a Trainee Editor Fellowship for the journal Anaesthesia,
reviewing over 40 manuscripts, contributing to the Editorial Board and
presenting at conferences. I have experience as a peer reviewer for the journals
Medical Education, BMJ Open Respiratory Research and the Journal of the Intensive
Care Society. I have collected data for national perioperative studies (SNAP-2,
DREAMY and PQIP), and am listed as a study collaborator on 6 published
manuscripts. I hold several leadership positions, including Chair of the Pan-
London Perioperative Audit and Research Network, organising the Annual

secrc__001_t.text:
Scientific Meeting, selecting the projects supported by the network, providing
research support, and delivering the annual Academic Anaesthesia Regional
Training Day. I am the Equality, Diversity and Inclusion (EDI) Representative for
the Faculty of Intensive Care Medicine resident doctor committee, setting up an
EDI strategy and working on projects to tackle differential attainment. I am a
Fellow of the South Asian Health Foundation, undertaking projects analysing

I was the Academic Trainee Representative for the Imperial School of
Anaesthesia, providing a point of contact for academic trainees and organising
the North London Junior Anaesthetic Group conference.
Achievements
I have shown academic aptitude throughout my career, receiving a distinction
for my dissertation and an Upper Second-Class Honours overall in my
undergraduate degree, a Merit in Clinical Specialties and first place in a national
prize while at medical school. During clinical training, I have completed the
MRCP, a Diploma in Tropical Medicine & Hygiene, and t

he FRCA; been awarded
several competitive travel bursaries to support an anaesthesia fellowship in
Zambia; and received prizes for quality improvement projects. I have completed
a National Medical Director’s Clinical Leadership Fellowship at NHS England,

Impact of Fellowship
While still at an early stage of my academic development, I believe that my track
record of publications, collaboration, and skill attainment provides a strong

secrc__001_w.text:
4. Plain English summary of research
600 words
Background
Every year, 70,000 patients need breathing support on intensive care. This is
delivered through a machine called a ventilator. Treatment with the ventilator
requires understanding of patients and their disease. A clinical decision support
system (CDSS) is software that improves decision-making. It matches patient
and disease features to a database. Treatment options are then suggested to
clinicians. The NHS Long Term Plan has suggested CDSS should support patient
management.
Deciding when to reduce ventilator support is important. Leaving patients on a
ventilator too long can cause lung injury. However, patients must be strong
enough to cope without its support. Previous work has shown differences in
clinical practice when reducing ventilator support. There is variable success and
no agreement on best practice. This suggests CDSS for ventilator management
could be beneficial.
Our national survey showed CDSS are not used for ventilator management. This
is because there is limited ready-to-use computer software. The survey also
showed clinicians do not trust CDSS. This is because their decisions are rarely
explained.
Addressin

secrc__001_x.text:
g the problem
This research aims to address these two problems. First, by using computer-
based methods to find patterns from routine patient data. Hundreds of
variables from thousands of patients will be analysed. These methods are

secrc__001_y.text:
summarised as machine learning (ML). Second, new visual methods will be used
to illustrate the data. This will improve understanding, trust and use of CDSS.
Research plan
This research will be conducted in three steps. First, understanding the patient.
We will model a patient’s disease using ML. This will improve understanding of
when to reduce ventilator support. Predictions will be created for the patient’s
whole admission. The most important features for each prediction will be
shown. Models will be checked for performance and bias. We will confirm
performance is maintained in under-represented groups.
Second, developing visual methods, or visualisations, to display information.
Visualisations will be designed with clinicians who work in intensive care. This
will ensure they find the visualisations helpful. The visualisations will illustrate
predictions for each patient. They will display the most important features for
each prediction. Visualisations will be displayed through an interactive
computer system.
Third, assessing clinical impact. Effect on clinical decision-making will be
checked through simulation testing. Decision-making using predictions from
ML and visualisation

secrc__001_z.text:
will be compared with normal practice. These insights will
guide future research. This could include testing CDSS in intensive care.
Patient and public involvement
This project has benefited from continuous patient and public involvement. This
has shown this research has potential for significant impact. First, through
reducing variation in clinical practice. Second, by improving patient safety. And
third, by using visualisations to communicate with patients and families. It has

secrc__001_{.text:
also shown that this research will benefit the NHS. This is by reducing length of
stay and cost of care. It addresses two priority areas from the James Lind
Alliance. These have been arrived at through discussion with patients.
Sharing our findings
I will share our findings through local scientific meetings and conferences.
Results will be published in academic journals. We will use Imperial College
London and collaborator networks to promote our work. This will be visible to
industry, other research groups, and stakeholders. I have convened a patient
panel. They will help design and present our message to the public. This will be
supported by the Imperial College Science Media Team and public outreach
activities.
5. Scientific abstract
500 words
Research question
Can explainable machine learning derive trajectories for successful and
unsuccessful weaning from invasive mechanical ventilation? Can data
visualisation of such explanations aid clinical management?
Background
Invasive mechanical ventilation (IMV) is an important intervention in intensive
care, delivered to 70,000 patients annually in the UK. Ventilatory weaning is the
process of reducing and removing ventilatory support

secrc__001_|.text:
and shows variable
success, with significant heterogeneity in practice. Clinical decision support
systems (CDSS) are software that integrate patient data with a computerised
knowledge base to provide treatment suggestions. They can support delivery of

secrc__001_}.text:
personalised ventilation to optimise weaning. However, few CDSS exist, and in
feasibility trials, advice adherence is low. A national survey found a key barrier
to implementation is that CDSS are “black box”, failing to demonstrate rationale
for their decisions.
This project will apply novel machine learning (ML) approaches within large
longitudinal clinical datasets to delineate weaning trajectories in adults and
children. It aims to test whether explainable AI (XAI) can provide
understandable and transparent explanations of decisions and actions to
human, through novel data visualisation techniques.
Aims and objectives
1. Iteratively develop and validate XAI-based models to delineate subgroups
based on physiological trajectories.
2. Develop and optimise data visualisation approaches to improve model
transparency for clinical application.
3. Validate data visualisation approach using simulation.
Methods
I will use a novel ML approach to generate predictions, using three neural
network architectures (Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT); Temporal
Convolutional Networks (TCN); Long-Short Term Memory Nets (LSTM)) designed
for processing sequential, time-series data to provide multi-hori

secrc__001_~.text:
zon forecasts.
This architecture will be applied to large longitudinal clinical datasets, totalling
over 100,000 patients. An iterative, expert-in-the-loop approach will interrogate
the models for bias, validate results and refine forecasts.

secrc__001_.text:
Clinician and patient focus groups and co-design workshops will be conducted
to understand stakeholder requirements and develop novel data visualisations
utilising a web front-end to display the results, displayed through the Data
Observatory at the Imperial Data Science Institute. These will be optimised for
clinical use through iterative co-design cycles.
Influence on clinical practice and decision confidence will be assessed with a
human-AI interaction vignette study using a modified, between-subjects design.
Think-aloud techniques will be used to understand rationale for decisions.
Timelines for delivery
I expect to develop and validate XAI-based models in months 1-12; conduct
focus and co-design workshops to develop and optimise visualisations in
months 6-27; and conduct simulations in months 27-33. I will write up my thesis
in the final 6 months of the Fellowship.
Anticipated impact and dissemination
This research will have significant impact. Patients will benefit through receipt
of efficacious, evidence-based therapy, clinicians through reduced
heterogeneity in practice, and the NHS through optimisation of patient time on
the ventilator, reducing length and cost of admissio

secrc__001_.text:
n.
Our findings will be disseminated through local scientific meetings,
conferences, and publications. I will utilise established collaborator networks of
key stakeholders to identify shared learning. I have convened a patient and
family advisory group who will assist with co-presenting our message for public
dissemination.

secrc__001_.text:
6. Detailed research plan
5000 words
1. What is the problem being addressed?
Clinical Need:
Invasive mechanical ventilation (IMV) is a key supportive therapy for patients
on intensive care1. In the UK, over 54,000 adults and 13,000 children
required IMV in 20212,3. Ventilation decision-making is complex, involving
integration of clinical data, setting initial parameters, titrating settings to
attain physiological targets, and determining when to reduce ventilator
support (“wean”). While several advances have been made in the field4–6,
there is significant heterogeneity in ventilation practice7. The goal is to
deliver personalised care, tailored to the individual8.
Health Technology Solutions:
Clinical decision support systems (CDSS) for IMV aim to enable personalised
care9. CDSS match patient characteristics to a computerised knowledge base
and generate patient-specific recommendations; they are divided into open-
loop (clinician input required to enact suggestion) or closed-loop (operate
ventilator independently)10. Currently available CDSS for IMV are
predominantly based on physiological models9,11 or rule-based
algorithms12,13.
Ventilatory weaning is the process of reducing and

secrc__001_.text:
removing IMV support as
patients improve. Our narrative review suggests weaning is where CDSS
may be most valuable, as it is multidisciplinary, there is significant
heterogeneity, and success rate is low14. It is a critical phase that every

secrc__001_.text:
ventilated patient must undergo; early liberation reduces the risk of
nosocomial pneumonia, ventilator-induced lung injury15, and prolonged
admission15,16, while premature discontinuation can cause fatigue, impaired
gas exchange and reintubation17,18.
A feasibility study testing an open-loop CDSS from our group showed
bedside staff rarely followed CDSS advice19. A national survey conducted in
response to this implementation gap found clinicians perceive the “black
box” aspect of CDSS as particularly troubling, with limited availability of
software that can be rapdily implemented in the NHS20–22. Moreover, while
CDSS for IMV have shown benefit in trials9,11–13, our survey identified clinical
use in the UK is limited.
Enhanced understanding and adoption through data visualisation and
explainable AI (XAI):
Machine learning (ML) can be employed to analyse historical patient data
and identify patterns that predict outcomes during weaning, though
previous studies have not employed time-series data23–25. Multi-horizon
forecasting (MHF) is the prediction of variables over several points in time. In
ventilatory weaning, a single-horizon forecast might predict settings for the
next hour, whe

secrc__001_.text:
reas MHF predict parameters and their trajectory over several
days or weeks, i.e., at multiple future time steps, providing estimates across
the entire admission to enable continuous optimisation of care.
Explainable AI (XAI) is the development of artificial intelligence (AI) models
that provide interpretable explanations for their predictions. In the context
of MHF, explainability is crucial for understanding the rationale for forecasts.
We believe that data visualisation, a technique used to generate hypotheses

secrc__001_.text:
from complex data26, can deliver on the implementation of XAI-based MHF.
Visualisation has been used to illustrate patterns in data, communicate
findings to expert and lay audiences27, and explore dynamics of Bitcoin
transactions28, online discussion29 and bio-surveillance30. Data visualisation in
intensive care has been limited to live dashboards and handover of care31–33.
The showcasing of patient ventilatory trajectory to augment decision-making
is novel.
Our group has applied XAI to illustrate feature importance when making
predictions. Analysis of SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values derived
from time-series models provided insights into which features impacted a
model determining patient trajectory in COVID-19 ARDS7; simple and static
alluvial plots were used to demonstrate how physiological trajectory linked
to patient outcome7.
Hypotheses:
1. XAI-based MHF models can identify weaning trajectories through analysis
of longitudinal clinical data.
2. Data visualisation can display these trajectories to augment clinical
decision-making.
This project will therefore aim improve understanding of weaning from IMV
using ML, creating MHF models that use data visualisation to im

secrc__001_.text:
prove model
transparency. This will reduce heterogeneity in weaning practice, augment
delivery of personalised ventilatory care, and increase the acceptability of
CDSS for clinicians.

secrc__001_.text:
2. Why is this research important in terms of improving the health and/ or
the wellbeing of the public and/ or to patients/ service users, carers and
health and care services?
This project tackles an important problem that is recognised by clinicians,
policymakers, and patients.
Ventilated patients represent nearly 40% of all adult2 and 60% of all
paediatric admissions to critical care3 (approximately 70,000 patients2,3). The
proportion of patients receiving invasive mechanical ventilation in critical
care is increasing34, and invasive mechanical ventilation remains the main
reason for patients staying on intensive care35,36. Mortality from conditions
such as acute respiratory distress syndrome1 and ventilator associated
pneumonia37 are high, and for patients who are ventilated for long periods,
the road to recovery is long and complex38. In this context, delivering
personalised ventilation strategies, that optimise patient time on the
ventilator is becoming increasingly important8.
Moreover, an increasingly multidisciplinary workforce require tools to assist
with delivering safe care39. The NHS Long Term Plan has called for the
development of decision support tools to help clinici

secrc__001_.text:
ans apply best
practice40, and the Topol Review outlined recommendations to use AI to
deliver personalised care41. Despite this need, our prior work has shown that
CDSS for IMV are rarely used in the UK, and that clinicians and institutions
are not ready to use these devices19.
This is a view shared by our public and patient representatives. I have
engaged members of the public through several avenues, including the

secrc__001_.text:
charity ICUSteps (https://icusteps.org/), and VOICE (https://www.voice-
global.org). I have presented twice to the Research Partner’s Group at
Imperial, used the Research Support Service, who also have lay
representatives, and the Patient Experience Research Centre, both at
Imperial, to present to groups with lived experience of both adult and
paediatric ventilation. The feedback has been very positive, with members of
the public agreeing that this was a very important research topic that could
have a substantial impact for patients and their relatives. The feedback has
been instrumental in reviewing and amending my lay summary, providing
feedback on the proposal, and highlighting outcomes that are important to
patients on the ventilator. For example, feedback has highlighted how data
visualisations could also be used as a communication aide for conversations
with family members and carers, helping to illustrate complex clinical data to
improve understanding and assist with achieving treatment concordance.
Principle outcomes of importance include reducing time on the ventilator
and improving comfort by weaning at the most opportune time, both of
which this research will address. Th

secrc__001_.text:
ey have highlighted the importance of
interrogating models for bias, maintaining confidentiality and provided
guidance for convening a Patient and Family Advisory Group (PFAG) as part
of the study group to ensure that our work remains of relevance to patients,
their carers, and members of the public. We will meet every four months to
discuss our findings and guide the project moving forward; they will also
assist with crafting our message for public dissemination and will be
involved in co-presenting our results at conferences. To further assist with
this, I have involved a public member co-applicant with experience of
participation in previous ventilation studies, who has been instrumental in
fine-tuning the lay summary, and will support the project through

secrc__001_.text:
participation in co-design workshops, assist with data analysis and
presentation of results at conferences.
In addition to these findings, this project also addresses two of the high
research priority areas of the James Lind Alliance, which were arrived at
through consultation with patients and carers (“What is the best way of
ensuring new knowledge and the latest defined standards are introduced
into clinical practice in a timely and effective way?” and “How can we predict
who will benefit from intensive care before admission and during treatment
in the ICU?”).
3. Review of existing evidence – how does the existing literature support
this proposal?
We conducted a narrative review to assess existing evidence, which has
been published in the British Journal of Anaesthesia. Our aim was to
understand the factors that influence clinical practice and decision-making
for mechanical ventilation, both with and without clinical decision support,
and the facilitators and barriers to implementation of CDSS. Our database
search identified 2,616 studies, of which 30 met the inclusion criteria.
Role allocation was relatively well described, with nurses most likely to lead
decisions around weanin

secrc__001_.text:
g readiness and failure; and physicians most likely
to lead decisions on initial ventilator settings, selection of weaning method
and extubation readiness. Interprofessional collaborative decision-making
was considered important, and is similar between adult and paediatric
critical care, but more common in western Europe, explained by differences
in role allocation, training requirements and cultural factors. Nursing

secrc__001_.text:
autonomy and influence on clinical decisions was dependent on nurse:
patient ratio and is more likely to be present when that ratio is 1:1. Unit
culture and reducing perception of clinical reprimand leads to greater
autonomy in clinical decision-making by junior staff42–51. However, no
information was found on whether this varies with units of different
leadership models, size, staff experience and composition.
There was no information on clinical workflow, though one study explored
reasons for delay in time to extubate, and no studies defined process
measures for ventilation, outside of morbidity, mortality, and weaning
success (which itself has several definitions). Arnal and colleagues
assessment of INTELLiVENT-ASV, a closed loop CDSS, and showed the
potential of these devices to augment workflow: use of CDSS significantly
reduced the number manual ventilator setting changes52.
Weaning of mechanical ventilation was explored in more detail, particularly
factors that influence nurse-led weaning. These are broadly grouped into
patient, nurse, and system factors49,53–61. In contrast, physician-led weaning is
poorly understood, with studies describing significant heterogeneity in
pra

secrc__001_.text:
ctice, and no evidence exploring impact of clinician specialty, experience,
or level of training. Physicians, while using a combination of subjective and
objective information, appeared more likely to draw on “gestalt”57,62–64. The
success of physician-led weaning is low, and reported to be less than 50% in
some studies14. Studies using vignettes to assess clinical decision-making
have shown significant heterogeneity in the information used to assess
patients, clinical practice for the adjustment of ventilator settings, and
limited between-clinician agreement65,66. The picture is further complicated

secrc__001_.text:
by the variability in definitions within critical care and those for treatment
criteria67.
No studies explored decision-making while using CDSS, though several
explored facilitators and barriers to implementation of protocols and care
bundles in intensive care; these are broadly grouped into those at affecting
the clinician, the intervention and the organisation20–22,68.
One study described factors to support implementation of CDSS; these
included integration with existing electronic health records and computer
systems, robust monitoring and evaluation, the presence of adequate
technical and clinical support and regular interrogation of clinician
interaction with the CDSS20. Other studies suggested unstructured
approaches to the implementation of care bundles68,69. No studies
documented use of implementation strategies or frameworks to guide
transfer to clinical practice.
Our national survey of critical care clinicians has highlighted that CDSS for
mechanical ventilation are rarely used in adult and paediatric intensive care
units in the UK. While most units have some form of electronic health
record, only two CDSS were used in intensive care, neither of which were
used often or ef

secrc__001_.text:
fectively. CDSS are not used for several reasons, notably
because decisions are not always explainable (“black box”), and a lack of
business case and evidence-base to support implementation. Despite this,
many believe CDSS could be beneficial for improving clinical decision-
making, patient safety and outcomes, as well as providing data for audit and
research, and acting as a tool to facilitate development of junior staff.
Several barriers to implementation were flagged, including resistance from

secrc__001_.text:
clinicians to changing practice, a perceived lack of benefit, deskilling of staff
and device complexity.
The potential for subgroup identification using clinical and plasma
biomarker data has been illustrated by the identification of the hyper- and
hypo-inflammatory subgroups in patients with acute respiratory distress
syndrome (ARDS)70, with its potential for therapeutic applications shown in
several retrospective analyses71,72. Physiological optimisation based on these
disease state has also been shown to be effective; patients with diffuse,
hyper-inflammatory ARDS are more likely to benefit from higher positive end
expiratory pressure (PEEP)73, reducing dynamic strain and atelectotrauma74,
while those with focal, hypo-inflammatory ARDS benefit from lower PEEP,
thus mitigating over-distension of the lung and haemodynamic changes
from ventilation75. In patients with the most severe ARDS, higher PEEP was
associated with better survival76, while ventilator strategy misaligned to lung
morphology was associated with worse survival77. In this context, optimising
ventilator management through improving our understanding of clinical
workflow, mechanical ventilation practice and disease s

secrc__001_.text:
tate is key to
improving patient management.
Moreover, a study of patients with COVID-19 induced ARDS using
longitudinal as well as cross-sectional data identified clinical trajectories in
ventilation data that were highly discriminatory for patient prognosis78; this
approach has been used to demonstrate trajectory of ARDS patients (based
on groupings of hypoxaemia resolvers and non-resolvers) from the MIMIC-
IV dataset79. Further work is required to identify whether longitudinal
physiological data can further define groups of ventilated patients.

secrc__001_.text:
Our review of the literature and background work in this area has
highlighted a lack of understanding of weaning trajectories of patients on
mechanical ventilation. While several decision-support systems for
mechanical ventilation have been designed9,11,12,13,80,81, explainability of these
models is limited and hinders implementation. The work of our group has
shown that critical care units are not yet ready to accept clinical decision
support systems for mechanical ventilation19. Promising work has been
performed that has identified patient trajectories that are predictive of
prognosis. We therefore believe that this represents a significant
opportunity for research.
4. What is the research question/ aims and objectives?
Research question
Can explainable machine learning derive trajectories for successful and
unsuccessful weaning from invasive mechanical ventilation? Can data
visualisation of such explanations aid clinical management?
Objectives:
1. Iteratively develop and validate XAI-based models to delineate
subgroups based on physiological trajectories.
2. Develop and optimise data visualisation approaches to improve model
transparency for clinical application.
3. Validate dat

secrc__001_.text:
a visualisation approach using simulation.
5. Project plan
Objective 1:
 To identify subgroups based on patient physiology and response to
ventilatory and pharmacological interventions.

secrc__001_.text:
 To identify features that define data-driven subgroups.
 To define impact of features with short-, medium- and long-term
prediction.
All ventilated patients with sufficient data will be eligible for inclusion in this
study. Using a-priori heuristics defining weaning as the first discrepancy
between set ventilator and respiratory rate, which have been previously
piloted by our laboratory group, I will stratify patients ventilated for < 24
hours, between 1-7, 8-21, and > 21 days. Weaning outcome will be based on
heuristics from the literature (success – extubated for > 72h, unassisted with
supplementary flow or pressure support > 1 hour after extubation; failure –
reintubation or death following extubation).
Patients will be drawn from databases with data sharing agreements in
place: MIMIC-IV (40,000 patients), Guy’s and St Thomas’ (GSTT, 20,000
patients), Royal Brompton & Harefield (RBHT, 20,000 patients) and Imperial
College Healthcare NHS Trusts (ICHT, 20,000 patients), and aggregated data
from three paediatric intensive care units (PICUs) across ICHT, RBHT and
Great Ormond Street NHS Trusts (15,000 patients). Traditional power
calculations are not required for machine learning

secrc__001_.text:
analysis, as by definition
there is no specific hypothesis being tested; thus, size of subgroups and the
effect size are not known a-priori. MIMIC-IV is hosted on Google Cloud
platform as a Structured Query Language (SQL) database; GSTT, RBHT and
ICHT data are stored in Trusted Research Environments on secure NHS
Servers as SQL databases; and the paediatric ventilation data is stored in the
Big Data and Analytics Unit Secure Environment (BDAU SE) as an SQL
database. NHS data will be anonymised (MIMIC-IV is already anonymised),
and all data will be extracted into CSV files.

secrc__001_.text:
The first step will be curation of the datasets. Using clinical domain expertise
with support from my supervisors, I will aim to identify a set of core
variables that are present across all datasets, with relevant parameters likely
to include those in the ventilatory, physiological, and laboratory domains,
and patient demographic and admission information. This will ensure that
our analyses are performed on clinically universal, rather than site-specific,
features. I will undertake pre-processing and exploratory data analysis to
understand correlation, collinearity, missingness, and outliers. Each variable
in the databases will be interrogated to ensure data is consistent with its
label, has appropriate units, and temporal relationships are maintained, and
where possible, variables will be standardised and normalised. I will use
descriptive statistics and simple visualisations, such as histograms, to
improve understanding of complex data. Where significant data missingness
is identified, I will use imputation methods, such as parameter-specific time
limited forward imputation for time-series data. Noise and outliers will be
identified with domain expertise, removing impossible valu

secrc__001_.text:
es (e.g., negative
physiological values); if necessary, proximity-based (e.g., K-means) or
statistical methods (e.g., Z-score) will be used. I will use simple tests of
association, such as regression analyses, to identify initial signals in the data.
Initially, each dataset will be analysed individually, to account for variations
in populations. I will divide each dataset into training (80%) and test (20%)
sets. Using signals identified from regression analyses, I will first assess the
performance of simple machine learning architectures, such as Random
Forest or support vector machine. I will then assess the Temporal Fusion
Transformer (TFT), an attention-based deep neural network architecture for
MHF, which provides robust forecast performance, is explainable, and can

secrc__001_.text:
be used to derive patterns from time series data. Knowing which factors
(feature importance) and historical data points (temporal attention)
contribute to predictions will demonstrate rationale. For example, feature
importance will be demonstrated using SHAP dependency plots, which
illustrate how different features influence model prediction over time;
clinicians may therefore be prompted to weigh the significance of
parameters depending on point of assessment. For example, in the service
evaluation of COVID-19 patients conducted by our group, SHAP dependency
plots showed ventilatory ratio was the most influential feature contributing
to model performance at day 67. Hence, I will assess whether, and how,
ventilatory and pharmacological (e.g., sedatives) interventions affect
predictions. I will assess for commonalities in the identification of weaning
trajectories and feature importance across datasets; if appropriate, I will
combine datasets to create a universal model that can be applied to all adult
intensive care populations. However, analysis of paediatric and adult
datasets will remain separate due to differences in physiology and
ventilation practice between these two populat

secrc__001_.text:
ions.
I will promote human-AI collaboration through an iterative, expert-in-the-
loop approach. In MHF, domain knowledge is crucial for stakeholders to
contribute insights, validate results, and refine forecasting strategies. If the
TFT model does not perform as expected, I will combine Temporal
Convolutional Networks (TCN) and Long-Short Term Memory Nets (LSTM),
convolutional and recurrent neural networks, respectively. The final design
will minimise bias while maximising performance and avoiding over-fitting
to maintain performance.

secrc__001_.text:
Model performance will be assessed in terms of model accuracy (i.e., ability
to predict outcomes using a single day’s data, data from the whole
admission, and across different timeframes of weaning), sensitivity,
specificity, F1 score (weighted according to class weights), receiver operating
characteristic curve and area under the receiver operating characteristic
curve (AUC). I will perform 5-fold cross-validation and select the model with
the highest AUC. I will compare different machine learning architectures and
select the highest-performing algorithm. Models will be assessed for bias,
calibration in under-represented groups, and we will be vigilant for the leak
of demographic data (e.g., age, gender) into non-demographic data. I will
assess algorithms against international standards for ethical ML and ensure
reporting of results conform to SAGER guidelines and NIHR-INCLUDE
Ethnicity Framework.
I will collaborate with the Imperial Data Science Institute (DSI) for this
analysis.
Objective 2:
 To understand and incorporate key information required for ventilatory
weaning and decision-making.
 To understand user requirements for data visualisation and co-design
solutions.
 To d

secrc__001_.text:
evelop visualisations that display weaning trajectories and features
of importance.
 To co-design an effective user interface that enables clinical
interpretation of visualisations.

secrc__001_.text:
Communication of predictive value, and uncertainty, is critical when
conveying forecast reliability and limitations. Uncertainty arises from
inherent variability in the data, model complexity, and incomplete
information about future events. Metrics that may help stakeholders
understand possible outcomes and confidence associated with each will
include confidence intervals, prediction intervals, or probability distributions.
I will use visualisations to enhance communication of forecasts and level of
uncertainty.
To understand key information required for ventilatory decision-making and
design visualisations, I will undertake co-design82 workshops with clinicians
who work on intensive care and use the ventilator. These workshops will
follow published guidance and involving patients and clinicians
throughout83,84, and will be led by an experienced facilitator, collaborator Dr
Tim Stephens. The iterative process of development, testing, modification
and refinement will utilise findings from Work Package 1 to inform the
design of the early prototype with potential users. We will run three
workshops, using a series of co-design techniques to facilitate discussion
and elicit design input

secrc__001_.text:
from clinicians and patients. These will include
storyboard techniques (e.g. use of scenarios to illustrate how the tool might
work in practice) to test feasibility, display of visualisations for ventilation
data to test acceptability, and “think-aloud” techniques, where users are
asked to talk about what they are doing while interacting with the
visualisation to understand decision-making85.
I will recruit 20 clinicians from five intensive care units of varying
specialisation, including a respiratory failure centre (Royal Brompton
Hospital), a paediatric intensive care unit (St Mary’s Hospital), and general

secrc__001_.text:
adult intensive care units (Royal London Hospital, Hammersmith Hospital,
Kings College Hospital). Agreements are in place with all included units. I will
purposively sample to ensure a mix of sex, ethnicity, professional
background and clinical experience. Sampling from different units will allow
an assessment of unit-based practice, and how visualisations might be used
in different settings. While we acknowledge consensus may never be
achieved, we believe this number of clinicians represents an adequate
subset to sample based on prior work by our group. I will include our PPIE
co-applicant, as well as 2 representatives from our Patient Family Advisory
Group, in the workshops. We anticipate each workshop to last half a day,
and each will include approximately 7-8 participants. Our experienced
facilitator will ensure equal participation and a flattened hierarchy.
Co-design workshops will be designed with the assistance of the Imperial
Healthtech Research Centre and collaborator Dr Stephens, overseen by
supervisor Dr Ni. Activities and questions will be piloted before use.
Workshops will be video recorded, and field notes will be taken
contemporaneously. The discussions will be trans

secrc__001_.text:
cribed, coded and undergo
thematic analysis based on Braun and Clarke’s reflexive thematic analysis86
using a hybrid of inductive and deductive approaches. Data analysis will be
conducted by myself, Dr Stephens and Dr Ni, with identified codes and
themes compared for concordance or disagreement.
Feedback from the first co-design workshop will be used to design
visualisations (e.g., time series plots, scenario analysis charts, heat maps,
temporal attention plots, interactive dashboards) to display different aspects
of the model considered important for decision-making, supervised by Dr
Serban. Visualisations will be showcased through the Data Observatory at

secrc__001_¡.text:
the DSI, where a second round of co-design will be undertaken with the
same groups to showcase initial design and gather feedback, assessing
acceptability of the designs. The second round of co-design workshops will
be designed, performed and analysed in a similar fashion to round 1.
Following this, I will create a user interface (UI), using a system architecture
that incorporates and processes the data, and a web front-end to display the
results, built around free-lock pipelines with reusable components that
transform data into a desirable format (allowing each component to be
efficient)27,30. Tasks will be run in the background due to required data and
processing volume. Processed data will output into a front-end data store
(PostgreSQL, an open-source relational database management system); the
front-end UI will run as HTML5 and React.js JavaScript app. The UI will enable
interaction with and filtering of data, and will be presented to the co-design
groups using validated tools87, testing feasibility and optimising for use in a
clinical setting.
This approach will seek to establish a deeper understanding of acceptability
of the intervention, usability needs for the interface, an

secrc__001_¢.text:
d implementation
requirements. This collaboration will involve our NHS partners; Dr Serban
and the DSI to assist with visualisation design; Dr Ni the NIHR Healthtech
Research Centre (HRC) to assist with qualitative study design, analysis, and
UI development; Dr Tim Stephens as a collaborator with expertise in co-
design; and clinical engineering at Aalborg University to help with model and
UI design.
Objective 3:

secrc__001_£.text:
 To assess how visualisations alter clinical decision-making in a
simulated environment.
 To assess how visualisations affect decision confidence in a simulated
environment.
To assess impact, I will conduct a human-AI interaction vignette study for
clinicians that operate the ventilator using a modified, between-subjects
design. Those recruited will be required to have at least 5 years of clinical
experience on intensive care and regularly adjust the ventilator in clinical
practice. We will purposively recruit to ensure a mix of professional
background and experience. A power calculation has shown that we will
require x clinicians to demonstrate…
Each subject in the experiment will perform 16 trials, with the first four
being used as a training period and thus identical for each participant.
The subsequent 12 trials will comprise the main experiment; for each
trial, subjects will be asked to determine whether they would wean the
patient from the ventilator, and to decide on ventilator mode and
settings for the next hour. Trials will be selected from real-world patient
data from the analyses in Work Package 1, by Dr Patel and Dr
Ramnarayan, mechanical ventilation experts, and will

secrc__001_¤.text:
represent all four
ventilation time stratifications, thus ensuring a representative mix of
complexity.
We will use a multifactorial experimental design with three arms, where
each clinician will experience all three arms on different subsets of
patients. The three arms are: baseline with no additional AI information
(baseline); additional AI decision support system information (AI);

secrc__001_¥.text:
additional AI decision support system with explanation of AI decision
using data visualisation (XAI). Our primary measure of interest will be
difference in ventilator settings for the same patient between the 3
groups, as a measure of the impact that the arm has on clinical decisions.
Clinicians will also be asked to engage in think-aloud techniques,
expressing rationale for their decisions and perceived confidence in
decision-making. Simulations will be audio recorded. I will employ
statistical analyses including two-sided T-tests for comparison of means,
and linear regression for assessing associations, and audio recordings
will be transcribed, coded and undergo thematic analysis based on Braun
and Clarke’s reflexive thematic analysis86.
6. Dissemination, outputs and anticipated impact
On completion of each objective, we will disseminate our findings through
local scientific meetings, national and international conferences (e.g., the
American Thoracic Society Annual Congress and European Society for
Intensive Care Medicine Annual Congress), and through publication. As part
of a research group which has published in high impact journals, and with
supervisors from diverse disciplin

secrc__001_¦.text:
es, I will be supported publish in peer-
reviewed general medical, health services and data science journals. I have
an academic track record of 12 publications, several conference abstracts,
and have co-edited and written several chapters for a medical textbook,
published by Cambridge University Press; additionally, my year working as
the Anaesthesia Trainee Editor Fellow has given me strong insight into what
is sought by editorial teams reviewing papers submitted to anaesthesia and
critical care journals.

secrc__001_§.text:
I will utilise established NIHR Healthtech Research Centre and Imperial
College networks of industry, clinician, research, public and patients, and
other key stakeholders to identify areas for shared learning (e.g., Severe
Acute Respiratory Failure Network and Imperial AI Network). I have
established collaborations with Aalborg University, the VentilaGroup study
team in Spain, and with UK groups, including two specialist severe
respiratory failure and long-term weaning centres. We have received
assistance from the Intensive Care Society, the Paediatric Critical Care
Society and Critical Care Research Group and will continue to use these
networks to disseminate our work. Our research group at Imperial also has
a history of collaboration with several international groups. Work will be
shared the aim of receiving feedback and sharing methodology; I will ensure
reproducibility of our findings by sharing code through repositories such as
Zenodo and in the supplementary materials of accepted publications.
We will liaise with our Patient and Family Advisory Group to determine the
best methods for dissemination to the public, while asking them to review
and contribute to any written materi

secrc__001_¨.text:
al or media, and to co-present our
findings at conferences. We will utilise the Imperial College Science Media
Team to publicise our work, as well as disseminating through groups such as
COVID support groups and the British Lung Foundation. We will also make
use of public outreach activities through Imperial College London.
I anticipate this research will have significant impact for patients, clinicians,
and the NHS. Patients will benefit through receipt of efficacious, evidence-
based therapy at the right time, improving safety and reducing ventilator
complications. Clinicians will benefit through improved understanding of
ventilatory weaning, thereby reducing heterogeneity in practice, and

secrc__001_©.text:
improving clinical training, shared learning, and communication with
patients and families. Finally, the NHS will benefit through optimisation of
patient time on the ventilator, reducing length of stay and cost of care.
7. Project management
I will be working full-time on this research project, under the supervision of
Dr Brijesh Patel, Clinical Senior Lecturer in cardiothoracic critical care within
the Anaesthesia, Pain Medicine and Intensive Care (APMIC) section of the
Department of Surgery and Cancer, Faculty of Medicine at Imperial College
London, with whom I will have weekly meetings to monitor progress, and
who will maintain high-level oversight and guidance.
I will be co-supervised by Dr Padmanabhan Ramnarayan, Clinical Reader in
Paediatric Critical Care within APMIC at Imperial, who will provide guidance
on paediatric ventilation and lend his experience of clinical informatics and
artificial intelligence; Dr Melody Ni, decision analyst and methodologist and
Deputy Director of the NIHR Healthtech Research Centre (HRC) at Imperial,
who will be responsible for offering supervision on the qualitative,
methodological and implementation aspects, has expertise in developing
method

secrc__001_ª.text:
s to translate technology into clinical practice, and provides links to
NIHR centres, including HRCs; and Dr Ovidiu Serban, Research Fellow at the
Imperial Data Science Institute (DSI), who has expertise in machine learning
and largescale data visualisation and will provide a link to the Data
Observatory at the DSI.
Additionally, I will be supported by collaborators including Professor Steve
Rees, Professor of Respiratory Critical Care Technology at Aalborg
University, who brings expertise in clinical decision support systems and
biomedical engineering; Dr Tim Stephens, Clinical Lecture and

secrc__001_«.text:
Implementation Scientist at Queen Mary University of London, who brings
expertise in qualitative research and methodology; and NHS partners,
including Dr TC Aw, Associate Chief Clinical Information Officer for Clinical
Analytics & Informatics at the Royal Brompton Hospital, an expert in real-
world clinical data development leading to application opportunities for
research, digital technology development, decision-making support
algorithms and training datasets for AI, and Professor Luigi Camporota at
Guy’s & St Thomas’ NHS Trust, who has extensive clinical and academic
expertise in mechanical ventilation.
8. Ethics
Several broad issues relating to artificial intelligence (AI) in healthcare apply
to this project. These include patient confidentiality, data security, data
transparency and accountability. In brief, this project is a retrospective
observational study. Data used for analysis has either been anonymised and
stored on a Google Cloud platform (MIMIC-IV), which meets required data
protection security standards; is pseudo-anonymised, stored on secure
servers with access obtained by following appropriate organisational
procedures, and will be anonymised prior to use for analy

secrc__001_¬.text:
sis (GSTT, RBHT
and ICHT databases); or has already undergone review and obtained ethical
approval for use in ventilation analyses (aggregated paediatric database,
IRAS 323325). I will apply to the Health Research Authority for approval for
use of NHS data, but following assessment with the online HRA tool,
Research Ethics Committee review is likely unnecessary. Patient consent will
not be required as all data is or will be anonymised. Data sharing
agreements are in place for all databases, which have undergone
appropriate processing for quality standards. Machine learning analysis
trains data on the whole patient cohort, so risk of identification is further

secrc__001_­.text:
reduced. Data at Imperial is stored on a cloud-based system that is
authenticated to meet the College’s standards for data security and is
compliant with UK and EU Data Protection Laws. To minimise risk of
corruption or unauthorised access, Jupyter notebooks will be kept within the
Imperial laboratory. All code used to extract and process data will be
maintained in Jupyter notebooks, stored in the online data repository,
Zenodo, and shared through conference abstracts, manuscript pre-prints,
and publications, improving transparency and allowing other researchers to
review and replicate our analysis.
Aware of the potential for AI algorithms to perpetuate algorithmic bias,
exacerbating inequalities by replicating established social and racial biases, I
will review the algorithms for bias and assess for calibration in under-
represented groups. I will assess the algorithms against international
standards for ethical machine learning and ensure reporting of results
conform to SAGER guidelines and the NIHR-INCLUDE Ethnicity Framework. I
will purposively sample for the focus groups to ensure a distribution of age,
sex, and ethnicity that reflects the population.
Alongside presenting to s

secrc__001_®.text:
everal public panels and seeking feedback from
the Research Partner’s Group, Research Design Service and Patient
Experience Research Centre at Imperial, and charities ICUSteps and Voice, I
have convened a 12-member Patient and Family Advisory Group, with lived
experience of invasive mechanical ventilation to advise on study design, data
analysis, ethical issues relating to our findings, and dissemination of results.
Their feedback has been very positive, with panel members agreeing this is
an important topic with potential for substantial impact; their feedback has
been instrumental in designing this proposal.

secrc__001_¯.text:
9. Success criteria
Success of this project will ultimately be judged by the dissemination of
outputs as detailed in this project plan, and completion of the doctorate.
Additionally, the success of each objective will be judged as follows:
1. Iteratively develop and validate XAI-based models to delineate
subgroups based on physiological trajectories.
 Identify and validate weaning trajectories using time-series
machine learning derived from routinely collected clinical data.
 Identify features of importance in the model and how these vary
with time.
 Successfully build a predictive classification model that can identify
short-, medium, and long-term trajectories from routine clinical
data.
 Demonstrate how clinical interventions (e.g., ventilatory,
pharmacological) affect predictions.
 Review model for bias and assess for calibration in under-
represented groups.
2. Develop and optimise data visualisation approaches to improve
model transparency for clinical application.
 Define key parameters required for ventilatory weaning and
decision-making.
 Develop visualisations that intuitively display data.
 Develop a user interface that enables interaction with the
data.
 Optimi

secrc__001_°.text: se for clinical application through stakeholder review.

secrc__001_±.text:
3. Validate data visualisation approach and investigate factors
required for clinical implementation.
 Assess impact of XAI with visualisation on clinical decision-
making when compared with normal practice and XAI
suggestion without visualisation.
 Assess impact of XAI with visualisation on decision-making
confidence.
10.Risk mitigation
One of the major problems with using retrospective observational data
relates to data quality. MIMIC-IV is a high granularity database that is
accepted as a reliable data source in the literature, with good
documentation and harmonisation between datasets, and over 2,000
publications to date. All code used for generating tables for analysis has
been made publicly available through Github (https://github.com/MIT-LCP).
The ICHT, RBHT, GSTT and paediatric critical care databases have undergone
appropriate processing for quality standards and where further data is
required these methods will be followed; database curation for the NHS
databases has been costed into this PhD application.
The application and testing of three neural network architectures (Temporal
Fusion Transformer, Temporal Convolutional Networks and Long-Short Term
Memory Nets) to ide

secrc__001_².text:
ntify weaning trajectories will mitigate against the risk
of model failure or poor performance.
The involvement of supervisors and collaborators from several disciplines
should mitigate against failure to complete the research objectives.

secrc__001_³.text:
7. Patient and public involvement
Please describe how patients/ services users, carers and the public
have been involved in developing this proposal. (350 words)
I have engaged members of the public through the charities ICUSteps
(https://icusteps.org/) and VOICE (https://www.voice-global.org), and
through Imperial College London, presenting at the Research Partner’s
Group (RPG) and the Patient Experience Research Centre (PERC), to those
with lived experience of adult and paediatric ventilation. I have received
feedback from the Research Support Service (RSS), who consult members
of the public. Finally, recruiting through VOICE and PERC, I have
convened a 12-person Patient and Family Advisory Group (PFAG), and a
co-applicant, Ms Joanne Lloyd, who will advise on the project.
Presentations to the RPG and PERC has involved 3 iterations of the
project; I presented to 6 panel members at the RPG, and 2 panel
members through PERC, who provided engaging feedback. All agreed
this was a very important research topic which could significantly benefit
for patients and the NHS. This was reflected in feedback from the RSS,
which highlighted the potential to reduce variation in clinical practice.

secrc__001_´.text:
The lay summary initially contained jargon and objectives were unclear;
after the input of the RPG, PERC and the PFAG, it was considered to have
captured the project’s main themes and was easy to understand. The lay
summary was also sent to members of the charity ICUSteps, and
feedback was very positive, with one respondent commenting, “It’s clear
what the research wants to look at, why they are doing it and how they

secrc__001_µ.text:
will do it. It is particularly encouraging to see the development of the
need to understand the patient as an individual.”
Following presentations to the RPG and PERC, themes to emerge from
the discussion were:
 Potential for artificial intelligence to perpetuate bias.
 Data security and maintaining confidentiality.
 Importance of transparency when reporting results.
 Ventilation outcomes that are important to patients.
 Potential for visualisations to communicate complex information to
patients and family members.
 Guidance on recruiting lay panel:
o Importance of including panel members with lived
experience of intensive care.
o Ensuring diversity amongst panel members.
o Ensuring appropriate safeguards in place.
This has led to amendment of this proposal, and following discussion, we
allayed these concerns and recruited the PFAG.
Please describe the ways in which patients/ service users, carers and
the public will be actively involved in the proposed research,
including any training and support provided. (350 words)
I have convened a PFAG to advise on the project. Feedback from the RPG
was instrumental for reducing barriers to participation; where I had
initially considere

secrc__001_¶.text: d good written and spoken communication skills, and

secrc__001_·.text:
recruiting on a first come, first served basis, the RPG suggested this could
led to a homogenous group and exclude certain voices. Subsequently,
criteria for recruitment were that participants should have lived
experience of intensive care, either as a patient or family member, should
be in stable recovery of at least 3 months since ICU contact; and > 18
years old. The group we have purposively convened consists of 12
people, age range 34-74, 75% female, 25% non-white, from all over the
UK, and with lived experience of adult and paediatric ventilation.
Their feedback has been instrumental in developing the proposal. We will
meet at least four-monthly over video conference to discuss our findings
and guide the project. The PFAG will also assist with crafting our message
for public dissemination and contribute to written material. We will
support PFAG members with development opportunities through PERC.
One member of the group, Ms Joanne Lloyd, who has experience as a
PPIE member for other mechanical ventilation studies, has agreed to join
the study as a co-applicant. Her expertise will be invaluable in assisting
with co-design workshops, co-presenting results at conferences, and
ens

secrc__001_¸.text:
uring the patient voice remains at the forefront.
Additionally, we will use the Imperial College Science Media Team to
publicise our work, and disseminate through groups such as the British
Lung Foundation and ICUSteps to reach a wider audience.
For PPIE training and support, I will seek support from PERC, including
attending their 4-week Public Involvement Training course, and seek
support from my supervisors and collaborators, all of whom have
experience of involving patients in research. This proposal has also

secrc__001_¹.text:
costed the time of the PFAG and co-applicant in accordance with the
NIHR Centre for Engagement and Dissemination guidelines.
To evaluate PPIE involvement and capture its impact, I will
maintain all patient involvement in the PERC log and regularly
evaluate progress with the PFAG and my supervisors.
If it is considered not appropriate and meaningful to actively involve
patients/ service users, carers and the public in your proposed
research, please justify why. (350 words)
N/A

secrc__001_º.text:
8. Training and development and research support
1. Proposed training and development programme (1000 words).
I am an early career researcher, highly motivated to become an expert in
the field of invasive mechanical ventilation (IMV), using mixed
methodology to develop clinical decision support tools using explainable
artificial intelligence (XAI). I aim to use the opportunity of this prestigious
NIHR Doctoral Fellowship as a springboard to launch a career as a
Clinician Scientist, lead my own research group and attain a Chair in
anaesthesia and critical care.
Supervision
In the short-term, I intend to complete a high-quality PhD under the
guidance of Dr Brijesh Patel and Dr Padmanabhan Ramnarayan,
physician scientists in adult and paediatric critical care and ventilatory
support; Dr Melody Ni, decision analyst and methodologist with a
background in translating innovation into clinical practice; and Dr Ovidiu
Serban, senior data scientist with expertise in machine learning and
largescale visualisation projects. The expertise in my supervisory team
will empower me to deliver on this ambitious project while acquiring new
research skills, disseminate my work through high-impact public

secrc__001_».text:
ations
and develop into an independent researcher. Gaining Fellowship support
from the NIHR will allow me to focus on these goals. Embedding within
the Imperial Data Science Institute (DSI), I will learn how to apply novel
time-series machine learning to understand weaning trajectories and use
novel data visualisations to deliver on the goal of XAI. I will obtain
support from the Imperial-NIHR Healthteach Research Centre (HRC) to
learn, develop and assess optimal approaches to improve clinical
implementation. This is a novel approach for producing XAI tools. I will

secrc__001_¼.text:
draw on my background in data science, the experience of my
supervisory team, and collaborators: machine learning (Dr Parbhoo at
Imperial), ventilation (Dr Camporota at Kings College Hospital),
qualitative research (Dr Stephens at Barts Health), and decision support
development (Professor Rees at Aalborg University), to deliver on these
goals.
Courses
I will attend local courses at Imperial College, accessed through the
Clinical Academic Training Office, who provide academic and pastoral
support for clinical PhD students, and host courses ranging from
statistical analysis to academic writing. I will attend the Public
Involvement Training course, run through the Patient Experience
Research Centre at the start of my Fellowship.
To develop skills in qualitative methodology, implementation science and
co-design, I have costed the “Qualitative Research Methods in Health”
course at University College London, “An Introduction to Implementation
Science” at Kings College London, and “Experience-Based Co-design”,
delivered by the Point of Care Foundation. Additionally, following
supervisory recommendation, I will attend the “Developing & Evaluating
Complex Interventions” course, delivered on

secrc__001_½.text:
line by the University of
Glasgow. This will be important for understanding how complex
interventions (clinical decision support for IMV) can be implemented in a
complex environment (critical care).
To improve skills in machine learning and coding, I have costed annual
membership for Coursera (www.coursera.org) and Datacamp

secrc__001_¾.text:
(www.datacamp.com). For example, on Coursera, the “AI for Medicine”
course includes modules on time to event modelling, useful for planned
analyses. Courses on Datacamp will be key for improving coding skills. I
will attend the “Mathematics for Machine Learning” from the MSc in
Computing at Imperial, which is included as part of Coursera
membership. Imperial College runs courses as part of the UKRI AI for
Healthcare Centre for Doctoral Training. I will utilise these to address
gaps in my knowledge.
As a basis for using visualisations to improve understanding of complex
data, I will attend the “Data Visualisation and Visual Analytics” course at
the Alan Turing Institute, which is delivered online.
International collaboration
I will visit Aalborg University to mature a collaboration with Professor
Steve Rees and his team. Professor Rees is an expert in biomedical
engineering, decision support systems and physiological modelling.
During an introductory visit in August 2022, we worked on a preliminary
analysis using physiological data to subgroup patients weaning from the
ventilator. We have subsequently worked on a narrative review assessing
clinical practice and decision-making in pa

secrc__001_¿.text:
tients requiring IMV (published
in the British Journal of Anaesthesia).
This visit, costed into the PhD, will be key for developing skills in data
analysis, machine learning, and decision support, enriching my
development and that of our critical care research group in London, while
fostering ongoing academic relationships.

secrc__001_À.text:
The future
On completion of the PhD, I will apply for a post-doctoral fellowship (e.g.,
NIHR Clinical Lectureship) to develop as an independent researcher with
a formal arrangement for postdoctoral training. Clinically, I continue
Anaesthesia and Intensive Care Medicine registrar training, where I will
work towards my Certificate of Completion of Training as a dual
specialist. I will continue to seek guidance from supervisors and mentors.
Long-term, I am motivated to follow a clinical academic track. I intend to
obtain a clinical academic faculty position in a university and associated
hospital, facilitating my research goals. At present, my academic interests
are focused on using machine learning to improve understanding of
ventilation and develop tools that improve patient outcomes, while
collaborating with laboratory scientists to understand the biological basis
for our findings. The field of data science in critical care is expanding but
limited work has been performed in ventilation. This Fellowship will
enable the curation of one of the largest longitudinal datasets for critical
care globally, enabling future analyses and creating an open dataset for
other investigators and c

secrc__001_Á.text:
ollaborators.
Securing this NIHR Fellowship will provide the necessary protected time
to immerse myself in this research. The Fellowship will offer accelerated
personal and career development to develop research skills, while
providing access to the NIHR infrastructure, such as career development
schemes, mentorship, and networking opportunities, aiding
development as an independent investigator. The Fellowship will allow
me to work within a world-leading critical care research group at
Imperial, while accessing collaboration opportunities. The support and

secrc__001_Â.text:
endorsement of the NIHR will be invaluable during dissemination of our
findings to the international research community, clinicians, patients, and
the public, which will enhance my clinical, data science, and academic
networks. All these experiences will strengthen my academic portfolio
and help with the transition to working as an independent researcher.
2. Research Support/ Supervisor (250 words).
Dr Brijesh Patel
Dr Patel was my supervisor during my NIHR Academic Clinical Fellowship,
and will be my primary supervisor during the PhD, maintaining an
overview of the project. He has a background in basic and translational
research in respiratory failure and mechanical ventilation, with an
interest in understanding the biological and physiological mechanisms of
failure to wean from the ventilator. He has been awarded > £5 million in
translational critical care grants, and is recognised as an international
expert in the field, with > 80 publications and several national and
international awards. He led the national service evaluation during the
COVID-19 pandemic, which used explainable artificial intelligence to
improve understanding of this disease. He has supervised 7 PhD
candidates

secrc__001_Ã.text:
, of which 4 have been awarded a PhD, 1 in write up phase,
and he is currently supervising 2 candidates. He has also led two clinical
trials (in collaboration with Professor Rees), on the implementation of
clinical decision support systems in critical care, which have led to the
development of this proposal. Furthermore, he is principal investigator
for a NIHR EME funded RCT examining mechanical ventilation strategies
in patients with severe respiratory failure supported on extracorporeal
life support, and studies examining ICU-acquired muscle wasting, a
critical determinant and consequence of failure to wean. I will meet with

secrc__001_Ä.text:
Dr Patel on a weekly basis, and he will provide support on all aspects of
this project, maintaining high-level oversight.
Dr Padmanabhan Ramnarayan
Dr Ramnarayan's research interests are focused on the use of artificial
intelligence to improve decision-making in critically ill children, use of
novel non-invasive imaging techniques such as lung ultrasound during
ventilation, and pragmatic clinical trials, particularly related to paediatric
ventilation. He led a master protocol of two national clinical trials on non-
invasive ventilation in children (FIRST-ABC) and he is currently leading a
clinical trial of respiratory support in acute bronchiolitis (BACHb). He has
an active interest in clinical informatics, which started during his MD(Res)
research conducting a series of studies examining the accuracy and
utility of a computerised diagnostic decision support system. He has been
awarded £4 million in critical care research grants as Chief Investigator
and ~£14 million as Co-Investigator, and he is recognised as an
international expert in the field, with over 150 publications. He is
currently supervising 4 PhD candidates and 1 MD(Res) candidate, one of
whom is due for completion in 2

secrc__001_Å.text:
024. Dr Ramnarayan will provide support
on the paediatric ventilation aspects of this project, which has
traditionally been considered separately to adult ventilation, as well as
lending his expertise on decision support systems.
Dr Melody Ni
Dr Ni is a decision analyst and a methodologist with over 17 years of
experiences in generating multi-dimensional evidence for the translation
of innovative ideas into clinical practice. Dr Ni is a deputy director of the
NIHR HRC in IVD at Imperial and has been leading a team of multi-

secrc__001_Æ.text:
disciplinary researchers since 2013, delivering impact through a range of
innovative projects, including the development of a breath test for the
detection of gastrointestinal cancers. Dr Ni chairs a regulatory network
for in vitro diagnostics, working with industry partners and key
stakeholders including MHRA and NICE. She has also hosted multiple
accelerator programmes aimed at building knowledge and expertise for
clinical scientists and innovators. Dr Ni has won over £10 million in grant
funding, recognised as an international expert in the field, with over 60
peer-reviewed publications. She has supervised 7 PhD students, including
2 as lead supervisor, of which 6 have been awarded a PhD and 1 an MD.
She is currently supervising 3 PhD candidates, 1 as lead supervisor. Dr Ni
will provide expertise and guidance on the methods and approach of the
project, both quantitatively and qualitatively, focusing on the real world
aspect of the technology and the value a solution can realistically deliver.
She also provides links to NIHR centres including the HRCs.
Dr Ovidiu Serban
Dr Serban is a Research Fellow at the Imperial Data Science Institute and
the Data Observatory group lead. His r

secrc__001_Ç.text:
esearch interests include machine
learning, natural language processing, affective computing and
interactive system design. His work includes real-time natural language
processing, data curation and large-scale visualisation systems. He holds
a joint PhD from INSA de Rouen Normandy (France) and "Babes-Bolyai"
University in Romania while working at the LITIS Laboratory in France. He
has over 40 peer-reviewed publications, including one best paper award
at a NeurIPS workshop. He has supervised 3 PhD students as lead
supervisor, of which two are close to their viva, and one as a co-
supervisor. He supervised over 40 MSc students at Imperial College, with

secrc__001_È.text:
over 70% receiving distinction or distinguished, including three students
being awarded president or department awards. Dr Serban will oversee
the project's machine learning and data visualisation aspects and provide
a link to the Data Observatory at the DSI.
3. Collaborations (600 words)
I have built several important collaborations. I will partner with the
Imperial Data Science Institute (DSI) on the data science and visualisation
aspects. Their aim is to advance research and create a community in the
field of data science; the unifying theme of their research is the pursuit of
innovations for building, exploring, and exploiting modern datasets. They
host researchers from multiple disciplines, including Biomedical
Informatics, Data Visualisation and AI System Architecture. Specifically, I
will work with Dr Ovidiu Serban, an expert in machine learning and large-
scale visualisation, and Dr Kai Sun, who has experience managing
translational research projects at the DSI. They will provide in-kind access
to the Data Observatory throughout the project for visualisation testing.
This collaboration supports two of the core objectives of the DSI
(developing data analysis technology that

secrc__001_É.text:
supports research and
translating data science into innovation through public sector
partnership); it also supports the DSI’s portfolio expansion in the field of
healthcare data, while leading to new relationships with academics from
Imperial College London.
I will collaborate with the NIHR Healthtech Research Centre (HRC), whose
aim is to support the development of medical devices, diagnostics, and
digital technologies, which help clinicians and patients to monitor,
diagnose and manage conditions including cancer and cardiovascular

secrc__001_Ê.text:
and respiratory diseases. Their multidisciplinary team includes experts in
clinical, technology, human factors and health economics, who drive
innovation, advance new technology and support healthcare staff to
reduce workload. I will be supervised by Dr Melody Ni, deputy director of
the NIHR-HRC. The HRC will also assist with building networks with world-
leading hospitals and clinical academics. My project will add to their
portfolio of innovation using artificial intelligence and the development
of decision-aiding medical devices.
I will mature an exciting collaboration with Aalborg University, which was
established during an introductory visit in August 2022. I worked with
Professor Steve Rees, Professor of Respiratory Critical Care Technology in
the Department of Health Science and Technology, on a preliminary
analysis using physiological data to subgroup patients weaning from the
ventilator in the Weaning Algorithms for mechanical VEntilation (WAVE,
NCT 03697785) study. Subsequently, we have worked on a narrative
review assessing clinical practice and decision-making in patients on the
ventilator (published in the British Journal of Anaesthesia). Professor Rees
and his researc

secrc__001_Ë.text:
h team have led the development of a model-based
clinical decision support system for mechanical ventilation, the Beacon
Caresystem (doi: 10.1109/EMBC.2015.7319595), which has been shown to
provide appropriate ventilator settings, reduce pressure support whilst
preserving respiratory function, and been the subject of several
international randomised controlled trials, during which they have
collaborated with clinicians at Imperial College London, including my
primary supervisor, Dr Brijesh Patel. I intend to mature this collaboration
during the PhD with a second visit to Aalborg, which has been costed into
this proposal, to develop expertise in data analysis techniques and
decision support; the Aalborg team provide expertise in biomedical

secrc__001_Ì.text:
engineering, physiological modelling, and decision support systems.
Aalborg University foresee several advantages to the collaboration,
including improved understanding of respiratory physiology, exploring
novel methods for improving explainability of decision support systems,
while fostering ongoing international networks.
Finally, I have several partnerships with NHS and university collaborators,
built during my Academic Clinical Fellowship, including with Dr TC Aw at
the Royal Brompton Hospital, an expert in real-world clinical data
development, Professor Luigi Camporota, who has extensive clinical and
academic expertise in mechanical ventilation, and Dr Tim Stephens,
Clinical Lecture and Implementation Scientist at Queen Mary University of
London, who brings expertise in qualitative research and methodology.
Their guidance and support will be key to ensuring the success of this
Fellowship.
